title: Re: MAP update — staggered handoff & late-binding governance
preview:
From: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>
To: Sarah Liu <sarah.liu@acme.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 14 May 2029 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: MAP v3 — potential procurement slip / proposed governance change

Sarah —

Quick heads up after this morning's sync with your procurement team: Tom flagged a new internal review of vendor contracts that will likely push signature by ~7–10 business days beyond our current close date. That creates two correlated risks for the MAP and the evaluation window: (1) delayed environment provisioning, and (2) a compressed eval cadence that could compromise the final acceptance criteria.

I want to propose a rapid light-touch update to the MAP so we can preserve the acceptance gate while acknowledging the procurement lag — two things we can do right away:

- Share a re-baselined MAP with staggered handoffs (allowing us to start evaluation on lower-risk routes before full prod access).
- Offer a temporary sandbox (pre-provisioned infra) for your SREs to run the first pass of latency/cost profiles during the contract review period.

I've attached the current MAP_v3.xlsx and our eval test plan so you can see the minimal-scope staging we suggest. If you can confirm procurement's expected sign date by EOD, we will turn around an adjusted MAP and a short governance note that documents fallback and credit terms.

If it's helpful, we can also route a one-hour canary kickoff with your engineering and procurement teams later this week.

Best,
Irene
Director, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference
Attachments: MAP_v3.xlsx (spreadsheet), eval_test_plan.pdf (pdf)

From: Sarah Liu <sarah.liu@acme.com>
To: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tom Hayes <thayes@acme.com>
Date: Tue, 14 May 2029 11:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: MAP v3 — potential procurement slip / proposed governance change

Thanks, Irene.

Confirming: Procurement has started an additional legal review that senior procurement expects to close around May 28 (they said 7–10 business days). That would move our signature target and likely the formal start of the full evaluation by one week. We want to avoid compressing the eval and losing coverage on the high-throughput scenarios.

We like the sandbox idea — if your team can host a pre-provisioned environment and provide the test harness, our infra team will run the latency/cost profiles. Can you outline the minimum data access/credentials required for that sandbox?

Tom — included for visibility on signature timing.

—Sarah
Head of Platform Engineering
ACME Corporation

On Tue, May 14, 2029 at 09:12 AM Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Sarah —
>
> Quick heads up after this morning's sync with your procurement team: Tom flagged a new internal review...


From: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>
To: Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 14 May 2029 13:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: MAP v3 — procurement slip (internal triage & mitigation ask)

Team — forwarding Sarah's note. Summary and proposed mitigations:

Issue: Procurement review -> ~7–10 business day delay to signature -> likely 1 week shift to formal eval start.

Proposed mitigations (high priority):
1) Sandbox provisioning (Redwood-managed) for ACME to run baseline latency/cost tests. Marcus: can you allocate two preemptible nodes in us-west for 3 weeks and a small KV cache snapshot we can mirror?
2) Staggered MAP: allow feature-by-feature acceptance — start with non-PII routes and synthetic traffic while procurement finishes. Jordan: need sign-off on the adjusted commercial milestone and whether we can accept phased acceptance.
3) Governance note & credit terms: propose a formal RISK entry and a fallback credit (one-week service credit if onboarding date slips >10 business days due to procurement). Grace: CS should own the acceptance gating and field technical Qs from ACME.

I'll draft the adjusted MAP and a succinct risk log entry (RISK-ACME-17) for transparency. Please respond with availability for a 30-min triage at 16:00 PT if we want to align with ACME before their Thursday procurement checkpoint.

Irene

> On Tue, May 14, 2029 at 11:08 AM Sarah Liu <sarah.liu@acme.com> wrote:
> > Confirming: Procurement has started an additional legal review...


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 15 May 2029 09:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: MAP v3 — procurement slip (internal triage & mitigation ask)

Irene / team —

We can stand up two preemptible us-west nodes by EOD Wednesday and mirror a ~10GB KV snapshot for the sandbox. Operational notes:
- Sandbox will have throttled throughput to match a conservative 50% QPS profile.
- We will provide a secure token with read-only access to the test harness endpoints; no customer keys will be stored in the sandbox.

Proposed adjusted MAP milestones (high level):
- Sandbox access & test harness: May 16 (sandbox ready)
- Phase 1 evaluation (non-PII routes): May 20–24
- Signature (expected): May 28 (per ACME procurement)
- Full acceptance window: June 3–7

Attaching MAP_v3_adjusted.xlsx with the new dates and acceptance gates. Jordan — commercial OK? Priya — please add the risk log entry (RISK-ACME-17) and tag the severity as "medium"; we'll revisit if procurement pushes further.

Marcus
Senior Infrastructure Engineer
Redwood Inference
Attachments: MAP_v3_adjusted.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>
To: Sarah Liu <sarah.liu@acme.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.ai>, Tom Hayes <thayes@acme.com>
Date: Thu, 16 May 2029 10:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: MAP v3 — proposed re-baseline & sandbox plan (action required)

Sarah —

Thanks for the visibility. We talked internally and attached an updated MAP (MAP_v3_adjusted.xlsx) plus a one-page governance note summarizing the fallback policy and credit terms. Key points:

- Sandbox ready: May 16 (Marcus to share access tokens and quickstart).
- Staggered acceptance: Phase 1 (non-PII) runs May 20–24; Phase 2 (full routes) runs June 3–7.
- Risk entry: RISK-ACME-17 (Procurement-driven signature delay) — mitigation: sandbox + phased acceptance + 1-week service credit if onboarding is pushed >10 business days due to procurement.

Action items for ACME: please confirm sign-off on the adjusted MAP by COB Friday (May 17) so we can lock the sandbox schedule and tooling handoff. We can send a DocuSign envelope for the MAP amendment once you confirm. HubSpot link to the deal and the DocuSign envelope are below.

If you'd prefer a short call to walk through the phased acceptance criteria, Jordan and I are available May 17 at 09:00 or 15:00 PT.

Best,
Irene
Director, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference
Related links: https://hubspot.example.com/deal/98765 | DocuSign: https://docusign.example.com/envelope/EC-54321


From: Sarah Liu <sarah.liu@acme.com>
To: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tom Hayes <thayes@acme.com>
Date: Sat, 18 May 2029 16:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: MAP v3 — proposed re-baseline & sandbox plan (action required)

Appreciate the quick turnaround, Irene.

ACME confirms the adjusted MAP and the phased acceptance approach. We accept the sandbox on May 16 and will run the Phase 1 tests May 20–24. Procurement will finalize signature via DocuSign — Tom expects that to complete by May 28.

Please go ahead and send the DocuSign amendment for signature and schedule the kickoff for May 21 at 09:00 PT for the Phase 1 test run-through.

Sarah
Head of Platform Engineering
ACME Corporation

> Thanks for the visibility. We talked internally and attached an updated MAP...

title: Merchant Labs Summit — ground-crew liaison & flight ownership rollcall
preview:
From: Jon Hayes <jon@travelops.co>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Emma Torres <emma@merchantlabs.com>
Date: Wed, 15 Nov 2028 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Merchant Labs Summit - draft ground-crew manifest and flight ownership

Hi Priya / Ben / Marissa —

Attached is the draft passenger manifest and the initial flight picklist for the Merchant Labs summit (Nov 28-30). A couple of asks to get us to final: 

- Can you confirm seat priority for the Redwood delegation (who needs aisle/window/preference for business upgrades)?
- Who will be the ground-crew liaison on arrival (name + mobile)?
- Any delegate with visa or special-assistance notes we should flag?

Quick notes: we held a small buffer on the evening of Nov 27 to accommodate late arrivals; PNRs are provisional until we receive final approvals from Redwood for the delegates marked as flexible.

Attached: ML_Summit_manifest_v1.csv (columns: name, role, email, prefer_seat, pnr, carrier, notes)

I can push final itineraries by Thursday EOD if I get confirmations today/tomorrow.

—Jon
TravelOps (operations@travelops.co)
Attachment: ML_Summit_manifest_v1.csv


From: Priya Natarajan <priya@redwood.com>
To: Jon Hayes <jon@travelops.co>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Emma Torres <emma@merchantlabs.com>
Date: Wed, 15 Nov 2028 11:03:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Merchant Labs Summit - draft ground-crew manifest and flight ownership

Thanks Jon — attaching a quick pass with preferences. See inline comments in the CSV. TL;DR:

- I (Priya) prefer aisle and would take the earliest outbound; mobile +1-415-555-0123 will be the arrival liaison unless an ops lead is preferred by Merchant.
- Ben has a connecting leg and has asked for a seat closer to the front (window ok); tentative request for upgrade subject to budget.
- Marissa needs wheelchair assistance on arrival (flagged in notes).

A couple of operational asks for Redwood:
1) Please mark Ben Carter as travel-owner for any PNR changes after Nov 22 (Ben will approve card charges).
2) Please include Merchant Labs contact Emma Torres on any day-of arrival comms.

Also attaching my itinerary snapshot for verification: itinerary_priya_natarajan_v3.pdf

Thanks Priya — quick clarifications:

- I can take travel-owner for PNR changes after the 22nd. Will also be the fallback approver for any upgrade spends.
- Ben (me) needs a short connection in DEN; please hold aisle if possible.
- For upgrades: we have a $1,000 cap for discretionary business upgrades for this trip. If costs exceed that, please route to me + Priya.

Marissa — noted on the wheelchair assistance. Jon, can you confirm assistance will be coordinated at Merchant arrival door 3? That will help the on-site team route the shuttle.

To: Priya Natarajan <priya@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Emma Torres <emma@merchantlabs.com>
Date: Fri, 17 Nov 2028 10:20:00 -08:00
Subject: Updated manifest & provisional PNRs (please verify)

Hi all —

Thanks for the quick responses. I attached an updated manifest (ML_Summit_manifest_v2.csv) with PNRs populated for confirmed itineraries. Highlights:

- Priya Natarajan: PNR RDX9PQ, United 871 (SFO→DEN→BOS), aisle assigned, arrival liaison listed as +1-415-555-0123.
- Ben Carter: PNR RDX9PR, United 871 connecting to AA 143 (DEN→ORD→BOS), aisle held pending confirmation; upgrade blocked until Ben signs off.
- Marissa Cole: PNR RDX9PS, special assistance requested; Merchant arrival door 3 flagged.

Attachments: ML_Summit_manifest_v2.csv

Please reply with one-line approvals so I can ticket and issue e-tickets. If no response by EOD Monday (Nov 20), I will assume current allocations are approved and finalize.

—Jon
TravelOps
Attachment: ML_Summit_manifest_v2.csv


From: Priya Natarajan <priya@redwood.com>
To: Jon Hayes <jon@travelops.co>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Emma Torres <emma@merchantlabs.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Nov 2028 17:03:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Updated manifest & provisional PNRs (please verify)

Confirmed — final approvals below.

- Priya Natarajan: approve (PNR RDX9PQ).
- Ben Carter: approve seat/PNR but Ben will sign off on any upgrade charges over $1,000.
- Marissa Cole: approve and confirm wheelchair assistance at arrival door 3.

Jon: please issue e-tickets and share a one-page arrival play (pick-up point, contact numbers, shuttle schedule) to this thread and to Emma. Also, can you add a day-of WhatsApp group link for the Redwood delegation? Merchant prefers that channel for real-time updates.

Thanks — Priya

--
Priya Natarajan
Director, Customer Solutions | Redwood Inference
priya@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123
Attachment: itinerary_priya_natarajan_v3.pdf

title: Temporary SLA routing & escalation for BlueCore migration
preview:
From: Emma Rodrigues <emma@bluecore.io>
To: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
Cc: BlueCore Ops <ops@bluecore.io>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 08:12:00 -0700
Subject: Upcoming migration — P1 response expectations and temporary routing

Hi Jonas,

We have a planned data migration window on Oct 7–8 (overnight PDT) that will move a subset of our traffic to a new ingestion cluster. During prior migrations we've seen transient increases in request latency and a few brief P1s while backfills complete. Given the scale and the cross-region timing, our security and ops teams are asking for a formal temporary SLA routing plan from Redwood so we can sign off on the maintenance.

Can Redwood provide: 
- A brief written amendment that clarifies temporary P1/P2 response target adjustments for the Oct 7–8 window,
- A nominated escalation path and backup on-call contact (APAC overlap is limited on weekends), and
- A draft DocuSign version so procurement can approve quickly.

Attachment: bluecore-maintenance-calendar.ics (shows migration schedule)

Thanks — can you loop in support and SRE?

—Emma
Ops Manager, BlueCore



From: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
To: Emma Rodrigues <emma@bluecore.io>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 10:03:15 -0700
Subject: Re: Upcoming migration — P1 response expectations and temporary routing

Hi Emma — thanks for the heads up and the calendar.

We can absolutely put a short-term amendment in place for Oct 7–8 that defines narrow tolerances and a clear escalation ladder. Quick notes from my side:
- Redwood proposes a temporary P1 response target of 30 minutes (remains our standard 15m outside of the window) and a P2 target of 90 minutes for the migration window only.
- We will stand up an explicit cross-functional bridge and provide a backup on-call from our EU/APAC pool to mitigate the overnight coverage gap.
- We’ll prepare a DocuSign-ready amendment and a one-page runbook with the escalation points you can attach to your change approval.

Priya / Laura — can you draft the amendment and confirm the on-call names? Marcus — FYI for the account and procurement path (HubSpot ID BC-7890).

Jonas
Senior Customer Success Manager
Redwood Inference

On Tue, Oct 2, 2028 at 08:12 AM Emma Rodrigues <emma@bluecore.io> wrote:
> Hi Jonas,
> 
> We have a planned data migration window on Oct 7–8 ...



From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Emma Rodrigues <emma@bluecore.io>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 14:22:40 -0700
Subject: Re: Upcoming migration — P1 response expectations and temporary routing

Emma / team — attaching a draft for your review. Key points in plain language so procurement/security can quickly evaluate: 

1) Scope & window
- Applies only to the BlueCore production tenant(s) impacted by the Oct 7 22:00 PDT – Oct 8 06:00 PDT migration window (as per calendar).

2) Temporary SLA adjustments (migration window only)
- P1 (critical outage impacting production): target first response time = 30 minutes. Our team will aim to provide an initial triage within 30 minutes; mitigation work continues per standard incident playbooks.
- P2 (degraded service with significant impact): target first response time = 90 minutes.
- Post-window: standard SLA thresholds (P1 15m / P2 60m) resume automatically.

3) Escalation & on-call coverage
- Primary on-call: BlueCore Success team lead (Jonas Weber) during business hours.
- Migration bridge owner (Redwood SRE): Laura Bennett — will be the on-call bridge lead for the migration.
- Backup on-call: Priya Natarajan (support manager) with delegated APAC responder: Sohjin Lee (if APAC is required).
- Escalation matrix:
  - T+0–30m (P1) — Laura (bridge) + on-call engineer
  - T+30–60m — Priya (support manager) and Jonas (CS) notified; executive on-call alerted if unresolved
  - T+60m+ — trigger cross-org SWAT per our incident policy

4) Rollback & fallback policies
- If latency persists above agreed thresholds for >90 minutes, Redwood will execute the fallback routing to the pre-migration cluster or activate traffic throttling per the mutual action plan.

5) Post-migration review & credits
- We will run a post-mortem within three business days. If we do not meet the temporary P1 target during the window due to Redwood-operated faults, we will consider a partial credit consistent with contract terms (details in the attached amendment).

Attachments: redwood-draft-sla-amendment-v1.pdf (draft amendment for DocuSign)

Next steps requested: Emma — please confirm acceptance of the amendment text or call out edits by EOD Wed Oct 3 so we can route to DocuSign. Marcus — can you confirm procurement contact and whether we should copy legal in the envelope?

Thanks,
Priya Natarajan
Support Engineering Manager, Redwood Inference

On Tue, Oct 2, 2028 at 10:03 AM Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Emma — thanks for the heads up and the calendar.
> 
> We can absolutely put a short-term amendment in place ...

Cc: BlueCore Ops <ops@bluecore.io>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 03 Oct 2028 09:15:05 -0700
Subject: Re: Upcoming migration — P1 response expectations and temporary routing

Hi Priya / Jonas — this looks reasonable and aligns with our change control requirements. Two quick asks before we sign: 
- Add a one-line confirmation that Redwood will provide a named backup on-call contact reachable via phone/SMS (we need a direct escalation contact in addition to email).
- Please include a DocuSign envelope; procurement wants the amendment in DocuSign to attach to the RFC. Our procurement contact is: procurement@bluecore.io (please copy).

If you can get the DocuSign link to us by EOD Wed we can approve the change.

Subject: Proposal: HorizonEdge pilot — Optimize Runtime & Efficiency Tiering

Subject: Re: Proposal: HorizonEdge pilot — Optimize Runtime & Efficiency Tiering

Emily —

Thanks for the questions. Quick answers while Karthik adds the deeper quant/benchmark numbers below:

- 20–45%: Baseline is token-unit cost reductions from batching (amortizing fixed decode overhead) + KV prefix cache hit improvements for repeated prompts. The optimistic case includes safe quantization (int8) on eligible layers and model-specific cache hit rates we observe from similar customers.
- Pilot recommendation: Given your mix (short + long), I recommend starting with the Hybrid bucket. The Hybrid pilot lets us enable aggressive prefix caching for short prompts while also optimizing batch schedules for longer jobs to avoid token-surge latency spikes.
- Procurement/Legal: Diego — I can draft the one-page MAP and include the technical acceptance criteria (SLOs for latency, cache hit rate targets, tolerable accuracy regression thresholds).

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Emily Chen <emily@horizonedge.ai>, Priya Natarajan <priya@redwood.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 15 Sep 2028 10:41:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Proposal: HorizonEdge pilot — Optimize Runtime & Efficiency Tiering

Emily — quick technical note from the inference team on quant and batching recommendations for your T5-like generator: 

1) Quantization guidance:
- int8: usually safe for decoder-only stacks and many encoder-decoder variants after layerwise calibration. Expect a 5–8% throughput improvement and 20–30% memory reduction with minimal generation-quality impact for common NLG tasks (sampling/beam sizes typical). For fine-tuned models, we recommend a calibration pass with ~2k representative examples and a small human eval.
- int4: higher upside on cost but much higher risk for quality regressions. We only recommend int4 for non-critical embeddings or if you can run a fallback int8 path for failed queries.

2) Batching rules of thumb (based on latency SLOs):
- Short prompts (~60 tokens): prefer micro-batches (batch size 8–16) with KV prefix caching; this keeps p95 sub-200ms for most modern backends.
- Long jobs (~600 tokens): larger batch sizes (4–8 concurrent requests) but ensure we use dynamic token batching to avoid extremely large per-step kernel invocations.
- Our runtime supports hybrid scheduling: short-prompt prioritized lanes plus long-run batching windows to maximize throughput without violating tail latency SLOs.

3) KV cache expectations:
- If 25–40% of requests are repeatable prefixes, you’ll see the upper range of the stated savings. The cache hit->cost delta is non-linear: small increases in hit rate yield outsized token savings.

4) Acceptance criteria suggestions for the MAP:
- <10% delta in generation ROI (per your evaluation prompts) for int8 versus FP16 baseline
- p95 latency <= 350ms for interactive endpoints in the Hybrid pilot
- KV cache hit >= 20% within 2 weeks of rollout for short-prompt traffic slices

Happy to run a short calibration job on a subset of your data (we can do that under NDA). If you share a representative sample, we’ll run quant experiments and provide a small eval report.

—Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Senior Applied ML Engineer, Redwood Inference

Carlos — thanks for the quick turnaround and for sending the notarized material. A couple of items from procurement / AP that will determine whether we can release the PO from hold: 

1) Certified English translation: the notary cert is in Spanish. Our AP policy requires a certified translation of any notarial documents not in English. Please attach a certified English translation of the notary page or confirm if the notary will provide an official bilingual version.

2) W-8BEN-E completeness: on page 2, box 9 (Claim of tax treaty benefits / U.S. TIN) is blank — that is expected for most foreign entities without a U.S. EIN, but we need explicit confirmation in the signature block that Sierra Bridge is declaring foreign status and providing the CUIT as the foreign TIN. If you can add a short addendum (one paragraph) certifying the CUIT as foreign TIN and its format, that will help our tax team.

3) Bank routing: AP needs a cleared bank confirmation letter or a bank-attested voided check PDF for the beneficiary details you provided. That can be redacted to show only account name and last four digits if needed.

Priya — looping you in for AP documentation requirements and the translation vendor. Can finance confirm whether Redwood can accept a certified translation produced by an Argentine court translator, or if we should request a translated and notarized version from Sierra Bridge?

Carlos — once we have the translation and the short TIN confirmation addendum I will route to Karthik and legal for conditional PO clearance.

Best,
Alex
Senior Procurement Manager
Redwood Inference

title: Vertex Analytics — proposed ephemeral capacity & burst governance
preview:
From: Anna Zhao <anna@vertexanalytics.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@vertexanalytics.com, ops-team@vertexanalytics.com
Date: 2028-09-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Peak profile & burst expectations for Q4 pilot

Hi Evelyn,

Thanks again for taking the time yesterday. Attached is our Q3->Q4 demand profile and a short note from our infra team about expected concurrency spikes (see attachment: Vertex_Demand_Profile_Q3.xlsx). High level: we expect several short windows (2-10 minutes) where sustained concurrent token throughput increases ~6-8x above baseline during nightly batch jobs and model evaluation bursts.

The procurement team asked for an example of how Redwood would guarantee availability during those spikes — specifically, what a "burst" SLA looks like for a Dedicated pool, how evictions would work if we hit capacity, and whether there are credits/override policies for transient oversubscription.

Can you share a recommended approach and an estimated committed sizing so we can move to procurement?

Regards,
Anna Zhao
Head of Platform Engineering
Vertex Analytics

---
Attachment: Vertex_Demand_Profile_Q3.xlsx (spreadsheet, 120KB)

From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>
To: Anna Zhao <anna@vertexanalytics.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-09-01T10:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Peak profile & burst expectations for Q4 pilot

Hi Anna,

Thanks — got the profile and reviewed the short concurrency notes. High level proposal (quick summary):

- Dedicated pool baseline sized to steady-state baseline + 20% headroom for predictable latency.
- Ephemeral burst slab: a pre-warmed, reserveable pool that can be consumed for short windows (configurable in minutes).
- Priority queues + preemption tiers: production-critical requests are pinned; best-effort workloads move to preemptible nodes during contention.
- Transient crediting: a monthly buffer of burst-minutes that can be consumed without penalty; above that, overage billing applies.

Im looping in Priya (account) to help with procurement language and our capacity team to size the slab. Priya — can you re-open the attached spreadsheet with the day/week views and propose a committed slab size and suggested ephemeral minutes budget?

Anna — want to schedule a 30-minute sync next week to walk through the tradeoffs? I can share a draft amendment that shows how credits and evictions are handled.

Best,
Evelyn Hart
Account Executive, Strategic Sales
Redwood Inference
Phone: +1 415-555-0123

Team,

Forwarding Vertex's profile. They want a clear, conservative committed sizing + ephemeral burst policy for the pilot (Q4). Key asks from the customer:

- How preemption/eviction works and expected tail latency impact
- A credits model for short transient overshoots

Can Karthik/Ben/Marcus please propose:
1) Committed baseline GPU count (recommended VM / GPU type) for the Dedicated pool.
2) Ephemeral slab size (GPU count) and warm-up policy (pre-warm vs cold allocate).
3) Autoscale policy: scale up/down thresholds, cooldowns, eviction priority rules, and warm-up latency expectations.
4) Proposed monthly "burst-minute" credit bucket and billing guidance.

Please include rough cost delta vs baseline so I can include in the commercial.

Thanks — aim to get a draft to Anna & procurement by EOD Thu (9/7).

> On Sep 1, 2028, Anna Zhao wrote:
> > [original message and attachment omitted]


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-09-03T14:50:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Peak profile & burst expectations for Q4 pilot — sizing ask

Evelyn — quick sizing calc and rationale below. Numbers are conservative and assume A100-equivalent GPUs with our optimized serving runtime.

1) Baseline committed pool:
- Vertex baseline concurrent streams: 120.
- Typical per-stream steady token throughput: ~15 tokens/sec average.
- Baseline GPU throughput (conservative): ~1,200 tokens/sec per A100-equivalent for mixed-length workloads (with batching).
- Required baseline GPUs = ceil((120 * 15) / 1200) = ceil(1.5) => 2 GPUs. Add 20% headroom => 3 GPUs committed.

2) Ephemeral burst slab sizing:
- Spike target concurrent: 1,000 streams => throughput ~15,000 tokens/sec.
- Existing committed 3 GPUs cover ~3,600 tokens/sec. Additional needed: 11,400 tokens/sec => ~10 A100-e GPUs.
- Recommend ephemeral slab = 12 GPUs (round up for safety & room for non-ideal batching).
- Warm-up policy: maintain slab in a pre-warmed state for expected spike windows (customer indicated short windows); cost tradeoff: pre-warmed slab increases baseline cost but reduces tail latency. Recommend pre-warmed for pilot given short 2-10 minute windows.

3) Autoscale policy (high level):
- Scale triggers: 75% of slab utilization sustained for 30s => scale +1 GPU (fast path); if >90% sustained 60s => accelerate +2 GPUs.
- Scale-down: cooldown 300s per step; only scale down when utilization <40% for 5 minutes to avoid oscillation.
- Eviction tiers:
  - Tier 0: Pinned production (no eviction)
  - Tier 1: High-priority but preemptible on 60s notice (best-effort fallback)
  - Tier 2: Batch/eval workloads — immediate preemption
- Warm-up latency: pre-warmed GPU returns to ready state within ~15s; cold allocation + model load ~90-180s depending on model size.

4) Burst credits recommendation:
- Pilot bucket: 10,000 burst-minutes per month (each minute defined as 1 GPU consumed for 1 minute).
- Above bucket: billed at our overage rate (x1.8 of committed minute price).

Estimated cost delta (monthly, rough):
- Committed (3 A100-e): $X,XXX
- Pre-warmed ephemeral (12 A100-e, but billed as reserved transient capacity): $XX,XXX (pro-rated for reserved warm hours; if they only need pre-warm during known nightly windows cost falls).
- Note: we can offer a hybrid: pre-warm smaller slab (6 GPUs) + fast scale for the remainder to balance cost/latency.

I'll attach a quick calc sheet to show token/sec -> GPUs mapping. Marcus/Ben — anything to add on ops constraints (eviction behavior, throttling)?

— Karthik

Attachment: sizing-quickcalc.csv (derived from Vertex_Demand_Profile_Q3.xlsx)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-09-04T09:33:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Peak profile & burst expectations for Q4 pilot — sizing ask

Adding ops perspective — a few constraints and recommendations: 

- Pre-warmed slab: we can keep a pool pre-warmed but should limit to windows (cron) to avoid constant cost. For pilot, agree pre-warm for 1 hour around expected nightly batch windows.
- Eviction mechanics: we use graceful preemption with a 60s signal. For pinned production requests we do not evict; for tier 1 we send a 60s notice with an explicit fallback URL that will route to a smaller model (if configured). Tier 2 is killed immediately with a retry token in the response.
- Autoscaler tuning: avoid step-chase oscillation by applying a tokenized smoothing window (EMA of 45s) and minimum step size of 1 GPU. Also apply a hard cap on scale-up rate (no more than +25% of pool per 60s) to avoid noisy neighbor issues.
- Warm-up: model weights should be kept in GPU RAM for pre-warmed slab; we recommend 2 warm copies for multi-model workloads. Model load times might spike if they attempt simultaneous cold loads — this argues for modest pre-warm + fast-path scaling connector.
- Monitoring & alerting: propose a dedicated dashboard (tokens/sec, queue depth, eviction events, p99 tail latency). Include an automated fallback policy that will flip to a smaller model or to a hosted fall-through model if p99 tail > 2s for > 2 minutes.

Operational risk: if they want entirely cold allocation for ephemeral capacity, expect 90-180s latency during first spike; not acceptable for production-critical flows.

— Ben
Senior Reliability Engineer
Redwood Inference

From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>
To: Anna Zhao <anna@vertexanalytics.com>

Date: 2028-09-07T16:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Peak profile & burst expectations for Q4 pilot — proposed plan

Hi Anna — thanks for your patience. Apologies for the longer thread, we wanted to make sure ops & capacity weighed in. TL;DR of recommended approach for the Q4 pilot: 

1) Committed pool: 3 A100-equivalent GPUs (covers baseline + 20% headroom).
2) Ephemeral pre-warmed slab: 12 A100-equivalent GPUs, pre-warmed during pre-agreed windows (we recommend 1hr windows around nightly jobs initially). Can reduce to a hybrid 6 pre-warmed + fast scale for remainder if you prefer lower steady cost.
3) Autoscale policy: conservative triggers (75% sustained 30s, fallback & cooldowns as outlined), eviction tiers (pinned -> tier1 -> batch) and a 60s graceful preemption notice for tier1.
4) Burst credit: pilot bucket = 10,000 burst-minutes/month. Above that, overage pricing applies.

Operational notes: 
- Pre-warmed slab required to meet sub-5s tail latency for short windows; cold allocation will not meet that SLO.
- We will provide a dashboard with eviction events and p99 tail latency and agree on an SLO for the pilot (suggest p99 < 2s for production lanes).

Next steps:
- Priya will put this into a draft amendment (attaching Draft_Burst_SLA_Amendment_v2.pdf) with the commercial.
- If this looks good, can procurement sign off on a two-month pilot commitment (month 1 settle parameters, month 2 run at the committed slab) while we iterate on the autoscale tuning.

Anna — would you like us to book a 30-minute call early next week to walk procurement through the amendment?

Thanks — looking forward to getting the pilot live.

- Recommend ephemeral slab = 12 GPUs (round up for safety & room for non-ideal batching).
- Warm-up policy: maintain slab in a pre-warmed state for expected spike windows (customer indicated short windows); cost tradeoff: pre-warmed slab increases baseline cost but reduces tail latency. Recommend pre-warmed for pilot given short 2-10 minute windows.

3) Autoscale policy (high level):
- Scale triggers: 75% of slab utilization sustained for 30s => scale +1 GPU (fast path); if >90% sustained 60s => accelerate +2 GPUs.
- Scale-down: cooldown 300s per step; only scale down when utilization <40% for 5 minutes to avoid oscillation.
- Eviction tiers:
  - Tier 0: Pinned production (no eviction)
  - Tier 1: High-priority but preemptible on 60s notice (best-effort fallback)
  - Tier 2: Batch/eval workloads — immediate preemption
- Warm-up latency: pre-warmed GPU returns to ready state within ~15s; cold allocation + model load ~90-180s depending on model size.

4) Burst credits recommendation:
- Pilot bucket: 10,000 burst-minutes per month (each minute defined as 1 GPU consumed for 1 minute).
- Above bucket: billed at our overage rate (x1.8 of committed minute price).

Estimated cost delta (monthly, rough):
- Committed (3 A100-e): $X,XXX
- Pre-warmed ephemeral (12 A100-e, but billed as reserved transient capacity): $XX,XXX (pro-rated for reserved warm hours; if they only need pre-warm during known nightly windows cost falls).
- Note: we can offer a hybrid: pre-warm smaller slab (6 GPUs) + fast scale for the remainder to balance cost/latency.

I'll attach a quick calc sheet to show token/sec -> GPUs mapping. Marcus/Ben — anything to add on ops constraints (eviction behavior, throttling)?

— Karthik

Attachment: sizing-quickcalc.csv (derived from Vertex_Demand_Profile_Q3.xlsx)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-09-04T09:33:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Peak profile & burst expectations for Q4 pilot — sizing ask

Adding ops perspective — a few constraints and recommendations: 

- Pre-warmed slab: we can keep a pool pre-warmed but should limit to windows (cron) to avoid constant cost. For pilot, agree pre-warm for 1 hour around expected nightly batch windows.
- Eviction mechanics: we use graceful preemption with a 60s signal. For pinned production requests we do not evict; for tier 1 we send a 60s notice with an explicit fallback URL that will route to a smaller model (if configured). Tier 2 is killed immediately with a retry token in the response.
- Autoscaler tuning: avoid step-chase oscillation by applying a tokenized smoothing window (EMA of 45s) and minimum step size of 1 GPU. Also apply a hard cap on scale-up rate (no more than +25% of pool per 60s) to avoid noisy neighbor issues.
- Warm-up: model weights should be kept in GPU RAM for pre-warmed slab; we recommend 2 warm copies for multi-model workloads. Model load times might spike if they attempt simultaneous cold loads — this argues for modest pre-warm + fast-path scaling connector.
- Monitoring & alerting: propose a dedicated dashboard (tokens/sec, queue depth, eviction events, p99 tail latency). Include an automated fallback policy that will flip to a smaller model or to a hosted fall-through model if p99 tail > 2s for > 2 minutes.

Operational risk: if they want entirely cold allocation for ephemeral capacity, expect 90-180s latency during first spike; not acceptable for production-critical flows.

— Ben
Senior Reliability Engineer
Redwood Inference

Thanks for flagging, Ev. I took a quick pass and recommend we present Skylab with a structured concession that preserves unit economics while addressing their near-term budget need. My proposed commercial structure: 

Option A (preferred, minimal long-term impact):
- Year 1: 30% off list on compute/ingest (cap on usage tiers)
- Year 2: 20% off list
- Year 3: 10% off list
- $30k onboarding credit applied to first invoice (cap on PS hours)
- Minimum committed spend: $450k ARR equivalent (or 60% of their projected usage)
- SLA relaxation: allow a 6-month validation period with limited SLA credits (explicit clause)

Option B (if procurement insists on 40% Y1):
- Year 1: 40% off list, Year 2: 15%, Year 3: 5%
- Requires: prepayment for Year 1 or an irrevocable letter of credit; signed executive-side approval (VP/CFO)
- Requires: enterprise security addendum + audit window scheduling post-signature

Rationale: Option A keeps FY1 concession within a tolerable band and protects renewal economics. Option B is riskier but can be allowed with strict prepayment / escrow and exec sign-off. Marcus — can you run the margin impact for A vs B and share a lightweight chart? Marissa — I think anything >30% would be an exception requiring CFO sign-off; confirm. Priya — any onboarding capacity concerns if we commit to a fast ramp?

If we can get signoff on Option A we can push legal a clean order form quickly.

Ben
VP Sales, Redwood Inference

Date: Wed, 03 Jul 2028 09:00:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Packaging variance request — Skylab procurement timeline (budget constraints)

Ben — ran a quick model and attached the CSV (margin_impact_skylab.csv). Key takeaways: 
- Option A (30/20/10 + $30k onboarding): estimated 8.6% incremental margin hit over three years vs standard pricing, NPV positive assuming their usage meets the commit.
- Option B (40/15/5 + prepayment): estimated 13.9% incremental margin hit and materially increases cash risk unless Year 1 is prepaid or secured.
- Break-even sensitivity: if actual usage is 20% below forecast in Y1, Option B becomes loss-making in the first year.

Recommendations: Approve Option A conditionally. If Skylab pushes for Option B, require prepayment or an LC plus explicit clawback for usage shortfall. Also require that onboarding credits are time-boxed to 90 days and capped on hours.

Marcus Lin

preview:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Tom Sanders <tsanders@acmecloud.com>, Rita Gomez <rita.gomez@acmecloud.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 18 Jun 2028 17:12:00 -0700
Subject: Site onboarding: K8s ops handshake & storage commitments

Hi Tom / Rita,

Thanks again for committing to a Private deployment with Redwood. To make the site visit and cluster provisioning high-confidence, attached is our preflight template (redwood-onprem-preflight-template.xlsx) — please fill the highlighted tabs and return by Thursday. Two quick asks up front so we can propose node sizing:

- Confirm whether you plan to expose management plane via customer VPN only or allow a dedicated mgmt VPC peering (we prefer peering for automation).
- Provide the expected working set for model artifacts and checkpoints (GB) and whether those should be on encrypted blockstore or mounted NFS for shared workload.

We’ll propose a compact node profile that balances ephemeral compute for inference and an SSD-backed storage node pool for long-lived artifacts. If you have strict encryption-at-rest or FIPS requirements, flag that in your reply — that will change the blockstore recommendation.

Tentative next steps: once we get the template back, we’ll send a nodepool + storage proposal and a 2-hour on-site/walkthrough window to rehearse failure scenarios (simulated rack fail + volume detach).

Jordan Blake
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
jordan.blake@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0192

Attachment: redwood-onprem-preflight-template.xlsx (spreadsheet, 120KB)


From: Tom Sanders <tsanders@acmecloud.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Rita Gomez <rita.gomez@acmecloud.com>
Date: Tue, 19 Jun 2028 09:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Site onboarding: K8s ops handshake & storage commitments

Jordan,

Thanks — we reviewed the template and completed the basic inventory (attached). A few clarifications from our side:

- Management plane: must be reachable only over our VPN (no peering) due to existing networking controls.
- Working set: model artifacts ~650GB, snapshots ~1.2TB, plus 200–300GB ephemeral cache.
- Preference: encrypted blockstore (AES-256) with customer KMS; NFS is acceptable for checkpoints but we prefer a CSI-based PV setup for performance.
- Compliance: we have a pending SOC2 addendum request from Legal; encryption at rest + audit logs are a must.

Also: can you confirm whether Redwood will support scheduled test reboots and the simulated rack fail? We want to include a small window where our infra team can shadow.

Thanks,
Tom Sanders
Head of Platform, ACME Cloud
tsanders@acmecloud.com

Attachment: acme-onprem-inventory-20280619.xlsx (spreadsheet, 85KB)


From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 20 Jun 2028 11:38:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Site onboarding: K8s ops handshake & storage commitments

Irene / Jordan — quick internal summary and proposal notes based on ACME’s responses:

Quoted:
> Management plane: must be reachable only over our VPN (no peering) due to existing networking controls.
> Working set: model artifacts ~650GB, snapshots ~1.2TB, plus 200–300GB ephemeral cache.
> Preference: encrypted blockstore (AES-256) with customer KMS; NFS is acceptable for checkpoints but we prefer a CSI-based PV setup for performance.

Proposal highlights (high-level):
- Cluster footprint: 3 control-plane nodes (HA, non-tenant), 6 compute nodes (mixed CPU+4x A100/FP16), 3 storage nodes (ssd-raid1 NVMe, CSI LVM backed)
- Storage layout:
  * Long-lived artifacts: encrypted blockstore volume, replicated across 3 storage nodes (rbd/ceph or iSCSI+LVM) — present as RWX via CSI for checkpoint jobs.
  * Snapshots/backups: nightly snapshot to on-site archive (object gateway) + optional async replication to customer S3 vault.
  * Ephemeral cache: local NVMe on compute nodes, managed by node-local-cache with periodic warm-up from blockstore.
- Nodepool sizing rationale: separate storage nodepool to limit I/O noise for inference lanes; compute nodes optimized for sustained token throughput.
- Failure rehearsal: recommend a 90 minute rehearsal window during cutover — simulated rack fail where we sequentially cordon/drain and detach a storage volume, then validate auto-recovery and data reattach.

I’ve attached a crisp nodepool-proposal-v2.pdf (internal) — please review and call out any redlines. Key question for Legal/Compliance: if ACME requires FIPS-certified encryption libraries, we need to lock that before provisioning the blockstore.

Priya Natarajan
Deployment Architect, Redwood Inference
priya.natarajan@redwood.com

Attachment: nodepool-proposal-v2.pdf (PDF, 250KB)


From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Tom Sanders <tsanders@acmecloud.com>, Rita Gomez <rita.gomez@acmecloud.com>

Date: Thu, 21 Jun 2028 08:42:00 -0700

- VPN-only mgmt plane: understood and fine. We’ll bring the on-site engineer and run the agent bootstrap over your VPN gateway (we’ll provide exact IPs for allowlist testing).
- Storage: we’ll recommend encrypted LUKS volumes with customer KMS integration (AES-256). If FIPS is required, please confirm the requirement ASAP so we can switch to FIPS-compliant libs.
- CSI vs NFS: CSI LVM + RWX is our baseline for checkpoints; we’ll present NFS as a fallback for low-latency cross-node reads if you want it for the tail-end of snapshot restores.

Attached is a short operations handshake doc (site-ops-handshake.docx) that outlines the rehearsal playbook. Proposed dates for the on-site rehearsal: 2028-07-09 or 2028-07-12 — pick a day and we’ll lock an engineer.

Next actions for ACME:
1) Confirm FIPS requirement (yes/no) by EOD Monday 2028-06-25.
2) Pick rehearsal date and provide the VPN allowlist endpoints.
3) Accept the nodepool proposal or provide redlines.

If those are green, we’ll prepare a PoS (statement of work) and a short DocuSign for the reserved on-site engineering day.

Jordan Blake
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

title: Urgent: token queueing causing SLA risk for ClearWave (reserved capacity)
preview:
From: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@clearwave.com>
To: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.com>
Cc: ClearWave SRE <sre@clearwave.com>
Date: Thu, 14 Jun 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Severe token queueing and spike in tail latency – production impact

Hi Irene —

We're seeing a significant increase in tail latency and an uptick in token-queueing on our reserved pool starting ~08:45 PT. Several customer flows are seeing 5–10x worse p95/p99 compared to baseline. Errors are small (5xx retries) but the behavior is dominated by long in-flight token queues and delayed KV-cache hits.

The attached sample includes request IDs and a short trace (token_trace_14jun.log) — you can see multiple requests getting queued for >2.5s before first token. This is directly affecting our payments flow (timeouts in step 3). We have an active maintenance window tonight; this is urgent.

Can you confirm whether this is capacity-related or a runtime regression? We need an ETA for mitigation and whether Redwood will trigger fallback to a compatible lower-cost variant.

Thanks,
Elena Ruiz
Head of Platform Engineering, ClearWave

Attachments: queue_samples_20280614.csv, token_trace_14jun.log


From: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.com>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@clearwave.com>
Cc: Michael Grant <michael_grant@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 14 Jun 2028 09:28:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Severe token queueing and spike in tail latency – production impact

Elena — thanks for the quick heads up and the logs. I'm escalating internally now.

Immediate actions I'm taking:
- Looping in Eng/Oncall (Michael) to surface pool metrics and look for queueing hotspots.
- Asking Infra to check recent kernel changes / kernel-selection heuristics and whether quantization variant routing changed for ClearWave's pinned model.
- Will propose a short mitigation: automatic fallback to the light-quant variant (if compatible) and temporary headroom increase for your reserved capacity while we investigate.

We'll confirm next steps within 30 minutes. Apologies for the disruption — we understand the payments flow sensitivity.

– Irene
Irene Choi
Director, Customer Reliability – Redwood Inference


From: Michael Grant <michael_grant@redwood.com>
To: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>
Cc: ClearWave SRE <sre@clearwave.com>
Date: Thu, 14 Jun 2028 09:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Severe token queueing and spike in tail latency – production impact

Quick update from Eng/Oncall after initial triage:

Observations (baseline -> current):
- Pool utilization: 78% -> 92% across the reserved cluster serving ClearWave.
- Continuous-batching depth increased unexpectedly by ~2.2x for short sequences (10–60 tokens).
- KV-cache lookup miss-rate jumped for a subset of model shards (see attached redwood-pool-metrics-14jun.png).
- A scheduling regression in the kernel selector (recent rollout, low-percentage) pushed a fraction of traffic onto a slower attention kernel for seq_len <= 64, increasing per-token compute and causing token inflight queues.

Immediate remediation attempted:
1) Rollback kernel-selector flag for the reserved pool (in progress) — initial rollout rollback rolled back 60% of affected hosts; still propagating.
2) Temporarily increased reserved headroom + enabled burst nodes for ClearWave (10 nodes added) — this cut median token delay but didn't fully clear p99.
3) Enabled automatic fallback route to a compatible quantized-lite variant (offered minor quality delta) — routing started at 09:40 PT.

Next steps: finish rollback, re-evaluate queue depth, and if needed, force-drain long-lived requests. We'll post a follow-up with clear timelines.

Attaching: redwood-pool-metrics-14jun.png and a short trace summary.

—Michael
Michael Grant
VP, Serving Infrastructure — Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>, Michael Grant <michael_grant@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 14 Jun 2028 10:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Re: Severe token queueing and spike in tail latency – production impact

Team — flagging this to leadership and sales ops. ClearWave is high-touch; they're on an annual reserved contract (DW-9821) with a payments SLA. Customer is already testing failover integrations so public optics matter.

Ask: Marissa / Irene — can you coordinate an executive bridge with ClearWave (CTO + our execs) at 13:00 PT today? We should:
- Provide a one-slide timeline of mitigation and ETA for full rollback completion.
- Commit to a short-term capacity bump or credits if rollback causes extended impact.
- Authorize Priority Incident Response (PIR) for billing/compensation review.

I'll prepare a short summary for the call and include suggested language for the CTO bridge. Priya — please capture any contract implications re: SLA remedies.

Marcus Lin
Account Executive — Redwood Inference


From: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@clearwave.com>
To: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 15 Jun 2028 16:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Severe token queueing and spike in tail latency – production impact

Thanks for the rapid responses and the bridge earlier today. The rollback reduced p99 in the payments flow to acceptable levels; we still see a small residual of queued requests during peak minutes.

A few follow-ups we need from Redwood:
- Final RCA including why the kernel-selector flag rollout targeted these hosts and why the compatible quantized variant routing didn't fully mitigate p99.
- A proposal for safe rollout policies to prevent similar regressions (suggested: staged kernel-selector tuning + synthetic pressure testing against pinned reserved pools).
- Confirmation of credits for the 4-hour impact window (we discussed PIR assessment on the bridge).

Appreciate the team’s help — please share the RCA and compensation proposal by EOD Monday.

Best,
Elena
Head of Platform Engineering, ClearWave

title: Joint action plan: security checklist & timeline
Action Required: Complete Third-Party Processing Map for HelixPay Pilot
preview:
From: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@helixpay.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@helixpay.com
Date: Mon, 20 May 2028 09:12:00 +0000
Subject: Initial ask: subprocessor roster + residency details for pilot

Hi Priya,

We're gearing up for the HelixPay pilot next month and procurement/security need the complete list of third-party subprocessors that Redwood will engage for the pilot. Specifically we need:
- Full roster (vendor name + country of processing + function)
- Whether any subprocessors will process cardholder or tokenized payment payloads outside the EU
- Confirmation of contractual mechanism for transfers (SCCs / adequacy / other)
- Any planned temporary access for non-Redwood staff or vendor support

Timeline: we need this packaged by EOD Friday 24 May to meet our review and legal signoff. Also include any evidence (SOC2 reports, KMS/HSM attestations) or a short plan to provide those artifacts.

Can you confirm? If helpful we can share our security questionnaire ahead of the call.

Thanks — Liam
Liam O'Connor
Head of Procurement, HelixPay
liam.oconnor@helixpay.com

---
Attachment: helixpay_security_qs_v1.xlsx (referenced)

From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@helixpay.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 20 May 2028 13:45:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Initial ask: subprocessor roster + residency details for pilot

Hi Liam —

Thanks for the note and the timeline. We're on it. High-level answers up front:
- We'll attach our current subprocessor inventory (redwood_subprocessors_20280520.csv) with processing country and function flags.
- For the HelixPay pilot, no cardholder PANs will leave EU data centers by default; tokenized payloads may be processed by analytics partners in the US only if you opt into cross-border processing.
- Transfers: we rely on SCCs for cross-border subcontractor relationships that do not have an EU adequacy determination.
- Temporary vendor support: we have a documented temporary-access policy; any third-party break-fix access is logged, time-limited, and requires prior approval from our security team.

I've attached: redwood_subprocessors_20280520.csv (roster + country + role), a DPA draft for your review (dpa_draft_helixpay_v1.docx). Ben and Rishi are cc'd for technical attestations. Marissa will take point on contract language.

Question for you: does HelixPay need SOC2 Type II evidence up-front, or would a SOC2 summary + targeted attestations (KMS/HSM scope + encryption in transit at rest) be sufficient to unblock procurement?

We'll plan to consolidate everything and send a single package by Friday EOD. If you can share your security questionnaire in the meantime that helps prioritize artifacts.

Best,
Priya Natarajan
Senior Manager, Legal & Data Protection
Redwood Inference

---
Attachments: redwood_subprocessors_20280520.csv (CSV, 29 rows); dpa_draft_helixpay_v1.docx (Word)

Liam / team —

Following up on Priya's note with a few technical clarifications from infra/security:
1) KMS/HSM scope: we use customer-isolated keys for Dedicated customers; for hosted pilot customers we offer envelope encryption where Redwood manages root keys in HSM. I'll attach the KMS scoping doc (kms_scope.pdf).
2) SOC2: we can provide the SOC2 Type II report under NDA; alternatively we can produce a redactable evidence bundle (key control, pen test summary, monitoring screenshots) to speed procurement reviews. Which do you prefer?
3) Temporary vendor support: any temporary access for debugging is executed via jumpbox with session recording. If HelixPay requires vendor access to logs or metrics, we'll put in place short-lived tokenized access and an access justification log entry.

If you have specific technical controls you want validated in the next 48 hours, enumerate them and I'll prioritize.

Ben Carter
Head of Security Operations
Redwood Inference

title: Request: temporary invoice-only onboarding for Procurely
preview:
From: Sophie Lang <sophie.lang@procurely.com>
To: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 14 May 2028 09:12:03 -0700
Subject: Quick question on PO requirement for our pilot

Hi David,

Thanks again for the demo last week — Procurely is excited to pilot Redwood for our internal knowledge-work automation. Before we finalize the onboarding form, our billing team asked whether Redwood can accept 30-day invoices without a PO for pilots under $10k.

We can provide a signed SOW and a W-9 immediately; the billing system we use is not set up to issue purchase orders from small pilots for 90% of our teams. If Redwood requires a PO we may need to route through procurement which could delay the pilot by 3–4 weeks.

Attached: Procurely_W-9.pdf, Procurely_SOW_Q2.docx (attach stubs)

Is there a temporary invoice-only path or an exception process we should follow? Happy to jump on a 15-min call to align.

Best,
Sophie Lang
Head of Procurement Ops, Procurely

---
From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 14 May 2028 09:18:40 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Quick question on PO requirement for our pilot

Team — forwarding Procurely's ask. They have SOW and W-9 ready but cannot issue POs at the team level. They want a short-term invoice-only option for a 90-day pilot (~$4.5k).

Key asks:
- Risk/compliance: what controls do we need to accept invoice-only for pilots?
- Duration cap: can Procurement approve a 90-day invoice-only window?
- Accounting: will Finance accept manual invoices for this?

Can Priya advise on approval lane and Laura on the accounting routing? Marissa — heads-up since this is a potential customer pilot.

Thanks,
David
Revenue Operations
Redwood Inference

---
From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
To: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 14 May 2028 10:03:55 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Quick question on PO requirement for our pilot

David — thanks for the context. Short answer: Procurement can approve a controlled invoice-only onboarding for pilots under $10k if the vendor passes our minimal evidence checklist and Finance agrees to temporary manual processing. Conditions I propose: 

1) Timebox the exception to 90 days (calendar) or first two invoices, whichever comes first.
2) Require signed SOW + W-9 + COI (certificate of insurance) or confirmation of vendor liability limits.
3) Add the vendor to a monitored allowlist with a mandatory risk review scheduled at day 60.
4) Require billing to include a unique invoice reference and remit-to address that maps to the HubSpot record (HS-20432).

If Finance confirms they can process the invoices and Legal signs off on the SOW language, I can approve an interim exception today. I will need a single approval ticket created in ProcOps with attachments (SOW/W-9/COI) and the HubSpot link.

Priya
Head of Procurement Ops

---

>
> I reviewed the attached W-9 and SOW. Two small asks: please have Procurely add an invoice line that references Redwood account ID and include the billing contact email (billing@procurely.com). Also we prefer invoices as PDF attachments to the AP ticket.

>
> Laura Bennett
> Senior Finance Manager
> Redwood Inference

- Please resend the SOW signed by an authorized signer and attach the invoice template you plan to use.
- Upload a COI or confirm waiver if you do not carry commercial insurance.
- Invoices must include: Redwood account ID, HubSpot reference HS-20432, invoice number, remit-to email billing@procurely.com, and be submitted as PDF to ap@redwood.com.

Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.example.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.example.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi_feldman@redwood.example.com>
Date: Thu, 09 May 2028 11:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Level & scope heatmap: Senior LLM Inference - alignment and interviewer focus

Quick read — I like the heatmap. A few bar-raiser notes and concrete interview-level guidance:

Red flags (immediate fail if present):
- No experience shipping at scale (ex: can only point to prototypes or single-node demos)
- Unable to reason about KV cache coherency / cold-start strategies
- No measurable impact on latency/cost in a previous role

Scoring anchors I propose (short):
- 1-2: cannot demonstrate production ownership or system thinking
- 3: solid tactical contributions with gaps in scale/design
- 4: clear owner of production systems, hands-on with performance tradeoffs
- 5: system-level thinking plus strategic tradeoffs and mentoring across teams

Suggested questions to surface tradeoffs (use at least 2):
- Describe a time you reduced tail latency in a production LLM workload — what did you measure and why?
- How do you design an inference cache that supports multi-version models and hot prefix reuse?
- Walk me through a regression you saw post-deploy and your rollback / mitigation decisions.

Debrief template (paste into shared doc):
- Key evidence observed (bullets)
- Risks / unknowns
- Recommendation: Hire/Neutral/No
- Level estimate + one sentence justification

I can take runtime/kernel questions and provide a 10‑minute deep dive if panel wants. Reserve me for those.

-Markus

Principal Engineer (Bar-Raiser), Inference Runtime

title: Renewal + Expansion QBR — coordinate roadmap demo & exec slot
Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up
preview:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rao <aisha@brighthealth.ai>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Apr 2028 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for taking the demo this morning. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we keep momentum toward a POC/PoV:

Summary of what BrightHealth told us on the call:
- Primary constraint: PHI-sensitive inference for triage and clinical summarization — must remain inside US with audit logs retained for 2 years.
- Target latency: p95 <= 200ms for single-turn generation (models that use a 2k token prompt).
- Current stack: mix of self-hosted Triton clusters (K8s + local NVMe cache) and some requests routed to another LLM API vendor (they didn’t name at first pass).
- Goal: reduce per-token unit cost while preserving deterministic tail-latency and KV cache reuse on long-running chat sessions.

Deployment options you asked us to clarify: Hosted API vs Dedicated reserved capacity vs Private VPC/on-prem. Below are our observations and a suggested sequencing for validation:

1) Hosted API (fastest path)
- Pros: fastest to stand up, we can demo with model pinning and per-route SLOs in ~48 hours; built-in prefix/KV caching and streaming enabled.
- Cons: needs VPC peering + encrypted transit for PHI; we can enable private endpoints but you’ll need an approved IP range and IP allowlist for the initial PoC.
- Fit: good if you can accept a private endpoint + platform-managed keys (we can also support customer-managed KMS for encryption-at-rest).

2) Dedicated (reserved GPU pools)
- Pros: predictable throughput, isolation, and capacity guarantees (monthly commit). Can attach to your VPC for egress controls.
- Cons: requires ~2–4 week lead time to reserve sizing and autoscaling policy tuning; pricing shifts to reserved-hour commitments.
- Fit: strong if you need consistent p95 latency with high QPS and want Redwood to manage runtime optimizations but keep network isolation.

3) Private VPC / On-prem (private control plane)
- Pros: full control of network, keys, and data residency. On-prem option can run in your data center (air-gapped) for audits.
- Cons: longest path (ops engagement + on-site infra validation), licensing & support contract required for private bundles.
- Fit: recommended for regulated production workloads that cannot rely on vendor-managed endpoints.

Migration notes (from your current vendors):
- From other LLM APIs: we can do a staged traffic cutover using model-variant routing + per-route fallbacks so you can compare outputs and tail latency.
- From self-hosted Triton clusters: we support importing model artifacts (if compatible) and offer quantization/compilation profiles that mirror inference behavior; for LLMs with custom kernels we’ll map differences and recommend a validation plan for output parity.

Immediate ask / next steps (proposed):
1) You confirm which two constraints we should prioritize in the PoC (e.g., p95 latency and PHI residency).
2) Marcus to run a quick sizing quiz with your infra lead (low-friction questions on peak QPS, avg seq length, KV cache hit rate, and allowed commit window).
3) Stephanie will send a one-page Mutual Action Plan (MAP) and a SOC2 evidence pointer for your security team to review.
4) If Hosted with private endpoints is acceptable, we can spin a PoC with synthetic traffic in 48–72 hours; if you need Dedicated or Private, schedule an ops scoping session next week.

I’ve attached a starter MAP and a snapshot of model comparison notes we discussed.

Thanks — speak soon.

Jordan Blake
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
[jordan.blake@redwood.com] | +1 (415) 555-0123

Attachments: BrightHealth-MAP-v1.xlsx; model-comparison-snapshot.pdf


From: Aisha Rao <aisha@brighthealth.ai>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: BrightHealth AI Platform Team <platform-team@brighthealth.ai>
Date: Tue, 11 Apr 2028 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up

Jordan — thanks, this is helpful. Quick clarifications from our side:
- We need PHI never to leave our VPC for the production workload. For PoC we can evaluate Hosted private endpoints if traffic stays in-region and encryption keys are customer-managed (KMS).
- Our infra lead (Vikram) will share peak QPS numbers later today. We are currently at ~1200 requests/minute during daytime with avg sequence length 1.1k tokens. KV cache hit is about 35% for conversational workloads.
- Regarding the other API vendor: we use them for low-risk features today but want to evaluate Redwood for deterministic latency and lower unit costs. We will likely look for an incremental rollout (10% traffic -> 50% -> full) over 4–6 weeks.

Please move forward with the Hosted private endpoint PoC plan while we validate legal and infra constraints; also send the SOC2 pointer to security@brighthealth.ai.

Aisha
Head of Platform Engineering | BrightHealth AI

On Tue, Apr 11, 2028 at 10:15 AM Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com> wrote:
> (original message)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rao <aisha@brighthealth.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>

text:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rao <aisha@brighthealth.ai>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Apr 2028 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for taking the demo this morning. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we keep momentum toward a POC/PoV:

Summary of what BrightHealth told us on the call:
- Primary constraint: PHI-sensitive inference for triage and clinical summarization — must remain inside US with audit logs retained for 2 years.
- Target latency: p95 <= 200ms for single-turn generation (models that use a 2k token prompt).
- Current stack: mix of self-hosted Triton clusters (K8s + local NVMe cache) and some requests routed to another LLM API vendor (they didn’t name at first pass).
- Goal: reduce per-token unit cost while preserving deterministic tail-latency and KV cache reuse on long-running chat sessions.

Deployment options you asked us to clarify: Hosted API vs Dedicated reserved capacity vs Private VPC/on-prem. Below are our observations and a suggested sequencing for validation:

1) Hosted API (fastest path)
- Pros: fastest to stand up, we can demo with model pinning and per-route SLOs in ~48 hours; built-in prefix/KV caching and streaming enabled.
- Cons: needs VPC peering + encrypted transit for PHI; we can enable private endpoints but you’ll need an approved IP range and IP allowlist for the initial PoC.
- Fit: good if you can accept a private endpoint + platform-managed keys (we can also support customer-managed KMS for encryption-at-rest).

2) Dedicated (reserved GPU pools)
- Pros: predictable throughput, isolation, and capacity guarantees (monthly commit). Can attach to your VPC for egress controls.
- Cons: requires ~2–4 week lead time to reserve sizing and autoscaling policy tuning; pricing shifts to reserved-hour commitments.
- Fit: strong if you need consistent p95 latency with high QPS and want Redwood to manage runtime optimizations but keep network isolation.

3) Private VPC / On-prem (private control plane)
- Pros: full control of network, keys, and data residency. On-prem option can run in your data center (air-gapped) for audits.
- Cons: longest path (ops engagement + on-site infra validation), licensing & support contract required for private bundles.
- Fit: recommended for regulated production workloads that cannot rely on vendor-managed endpoints.

Migration notes (from your current vendors):
- From other LLM APIs: we can do a staged traffic cutover using model-variant routing + per-route fallbacks so you can compare outputs and tail latency.
- From self-hosted Triton clusters: we support importing model artifacts (if compatible) and offer quantization/compilation profiles that mirror inference behavior; for LLMs with custom kernels we’ll map differences and recommend a validation plan for output parity.

Immediate ask / next steps (proposed):
1) You confirm which two constraints we should prioritize in the PoC (e.g., p95 latency and PHI residency).
2) Marcus to run a quick sizing quiz with your infra lead (low-friction questions on peak QPS, avg seq length, KV cache hit rate, and allowed commit window).
3) Stephanie will send a one-page Mutual Action Plan (MAP) and a SOC2 evidence pointer for your security team to review.
4) If Hosted with private endpoints is acceptable, we can spin a PoC with synthetic traffic in 48–72 hours; if you need Dedicated or Private, schedule an ops scoping session next week.

I’ve attached a starter MAP and a snapshot of model comparison notes we discussed.

Thanks — speak soon.

Jordan Blake
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
[jordan.blake@redwood.com] | +1 (415) 555-0123

Attachments: BrightHealth-MAP-v1.xlsx; model-comparison-snapshot.pdf

Cc: BrightHealth AI Platform Team <platform-team@brighthealth.ai>
Date: Tue, 11 Apr 2028 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Deployment venue and migration constraints — post-demo follow-up

Jordan — thanks, this is helpful. Quick clarifications from our side:
- We need PHI never to leave our VPC for the production workload. For PoC we can evaluate Hosted private endpoints if traffic stays in-region and encryption keys are customer-managed (KMS).
- Our infra lead (Vikram) will share peak QPS numbers later today. We are currently at ~1200 requests/minute during daytime with avg sequence length 1.1k tokens. KV cache hit is about 35% for conversational workloads.
- Regarding the other API vendor: we use them for low-risk features today but want to evaluate Redwood for deterministic latency and lower unit costs. We will likely look for an incremental rollout (10% traffic -> 50% -> full) over 4–6 weeks.

Please move forward with the Hosted private endpoint PoC plan while we validate legal and infra constraints; also send the SOC2 pointer to security@brighthealth.ai.

Aisha
Head of Platform Engineering | BrightHealth AI

title: Indemnity: escalation triggers and evidence staging for IP claims
preview:
From: Jordan Mills <jordan.mills@prospectco.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel White <rachel.white@prospectco.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Mar 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Potential IP scenario — how would Redwood handle 3rd-party claims?

Hi Aditya,

Thanks for the demo yesterday — momentum is good. Legal here flagged a hypothetical: ProspectCo embeds outputs from Redwood-powered inference in customer-facing features. If a third party alleges the output infringes their IP, what is Redwood willing to commit to for (a) defense and (b) indemnity?

Specifically:
- Who controls litigation (defend vs. reimburse)?
- What are timing/notice and evidence requirements (we would expect cooperation/hand-off of claim details)?
- Any exclusions (AI-generated content, open-source model issues)?

I attached a short scenario our counsel prepared. We need an initial position to proceed with procurement — timing is this week.

Appreciate a quick note on options and the playbook you typically use.

Jordan
Counsel, ProspectCo
Attachment: prospect_claim_scenario.pdf (1 page)


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jordan Mills <jordan.mills@prospectco.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Mar 2028 10:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Potential IP scenario — how would Redwood handle 3rd-party claims?

Jordan — thanks, appreciate the concrete scenario. Looping in our legal and procurement leads (David, Kimberly) and Priya on the commercial side.

Short summary of commercial options we commonly offer:
1) Defense-Control (Redwood leads defense): Redwood conducts defense subject to cooperation from ProspectCo; Redwood retains control but will consult on settlement decisions. Typical cap = insurance limit + negotiated contract cap.
2) Reimburse (ProspectCo leads/controls): ProspectCo controls the defense and seeks reimbursement for reasonable costs; Redwood still requires notice and right to participate.
3) Hybrid (defend for certain claim types; reimburse for others): e.g., Redwood defends third-party patent/copyright claims tied to the model weights or infra; claims about customer prompts/inputs or downstream uses are reimburse-only.

Key practical asks we need from ProspectCo:
- Timely notice (we ask within 10 business days of learning of claim)
- Preservation of evidence and handoff of relevant prompts/outputs (we will reciprocally preserve logs up to agreed retention)
- Right to defend or nominate counsel if Redwood is to lead; cooperation clause if customer leads

David/Kim — can you propose concrete redline language and preferred caps/exclusions? Priya — if we land on hybrid, what commercial trade (discount/credit) do we offer?

I expect we can give ProspectCo an initial compromise by end of day if legal aligns.

Cheers,
Aditya
Director, BD — Redwood Inference


From: David Kim <david.kim@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Mar 2028 13:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Potential IP scenario — how would Redwood handle 3rd-party claims?

Team — quick legal note / proposed redlines after reviewing ProspectCo's scenario: see attached redline snippet. Major points: 

1) Scope: Carve out \"AI-generated outputs created solely from customer prompts\" from Redwood's obligation to defend or indemnify. We can commit to defend and indemnify for third-party claims alleging infringement arising from Redwood-provided model code, weights, or underlying platform.

2) Notice and evidence staging: Require written notice within 10 business days; customer must provide prompt/output samples and any third-party complaint. Redwood to advise within 15 business days whether it will assume defense. If Redwood assumes defense, customer must provide a single privileged evidence package and a named contact for discovery — limit back-and-forth to preserve timelines.

3) Control/settlement: Redwood may assume defense with counsel approved by Redwood; settlements that require payment must be mutually agreed if they create ongoing obligations on the customer. If customer insists on controlling defense, Redwood will reimburse reasonable fees subject to review and the same evidence cooperation.

4) Caps / insurance: Recommend a combined approach — Redwood's indemnity limited to the lesser of (i) an order-form cap (e.g., $1,000,000) and (ii) Redwood's available insurance limit for the year. This preserves predictability.

5) Exclusions: No indemnity for claims arising from: (i) customer's modification of outputs after receipt, (ii) customer's failure to apply required mitigations, (iii) open-source dependencies for which customer took on risk per order form.

Attachment: redline_msa_indemnity_snippet.docx (2 pages) — includes proposed clause text and a short comment thread explaining tradeoffs.

If we want to soften scope for enterprise deals, we can trade additional assurances (e.g., expanded log retention, higher SLA credits) for a broader defense commitment. Thoughts?

-David
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jordan Mills <jordan.mills@prospectco.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel White <rachel.white@prospectco.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Mar 2028 16:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Potential IP scenario — how would Redwood handle 3rd-party claims?

Jordan — thanks for the patience. Attached is a short summary of our proposed compromise and the redline snippet David prepared. High-level offer we can take to ProspectCo counsel: 

- Redwood will defend and indemnify for third-party claims that allege infringement by Redwood's platform (code, model weights, serving infra).
- Claims that appear to arise from customer-provided prompts, post-processing of outputs, or customer modifications are outside Redwood's indemnity; for those, Redwood will reimburse reasonable costs if the customer elects to control defense, subject to a review and mutual cooperation protocol.
- Notice within 10 business days; evidence handoff (single package) and Redwood to accept/decline defense within 15 business days.
- Cap: Redwood indemnity limited to the lesser of $1,000,000 or Redwood's available insurance limit.

If ProspectCo needs the carveouts narrowed for enterprise procurement, we're open to discussing concessions (longer log retention, SOC evidence package, incremental platform credits) in exchange for tightening the AI-output exclusion.

Can you confirm whether this is sufficient for your counsel to proceed? If yes, we can send a one-page summary + redline via DocuSign for signature and advance to final order-form language.

Best,
Aditya
Director, BD — Redwood Inference

If Redwood assumes defense, customer must provide a single privileged evidence package and a named contact for discovery — limit back-and-forth to preserve timelines.
>

"
